title: Move Enterprise Plan From Seat-Based to Platform Pricing
question: Should we move enterprise customers from seat-based pricing to platform pricing this quarter?
context: Enterprise customers increasingly use the product across workflows, but seat expansion is slow and procurement conversations focus on total business value rather than user count.

recommendation.summary: Run a controlled pilot with new enterprise opportunities and one renewal cohort instead of migrating all accounts at once.

strategic_goal: Increase enterprise ARR while aligning pricing with customer value.
constraints: Existing contracts cannot be changed unilaterally.
Sales team needs a simple explanation and discount guardrails.
Finance needs comparable reporting across old and new plans.

financial_impact.revenue: Potential ARR uplift if platform value is priced better than seat count.
financial_impact.cost: Sales enablement, contract updates, billing changes, and customer communication.
financial_impact.cash_flow: Positive if renewals accept higher annual commitments; negative if migration delays deals.
financial_impact.payback_period: Target payback within two quarters after pilot launch.

milestones: Define pilot customer segment and pricing bands.
Create sales narrative and discount rules.
Update order form and billing workflow.
Review pilot conversion and expansion data.

hypotheses.statement: Platform pricing better matches enterprise willingness to pay than seat-based pricing.
hypotheses.why_it_matters: If true, the company can capture more value without relying on slow seat expansion.
evidence: Enterprise buyers describe value in workflow coverage and risk reduction.
Several accounts use shared operational workflows where seat count understates value.
assumptions: Buyers can understand and defend platform pricing internally.
The new metric maps to value better than seat count.
counterarguments: Seat pricing is easier to benchmark and approve.
Platform pricing could create procurement friction if value metric feels arbitrary.
disconfirming_signals: Pilot deals show longer sales cycles without higher ACV.
Customers negotiate back to seat-based pricing.

hypotheses.statement: A pilot reduces migration risk while producing enough signal for a broader rollout.
hypotheses.why_it_matters: Pricing changes are high leverage but can damage trust if forced too quickly.
evidence: A limited cohort allows sales and CS to refine messaging.
New opportunities are less sensitive than existing customers with anchored expectations.
assumptions: The pilot cohort is representative enough.
Sales will follow guardrails instead of discounting away the new model.
counterarguments: Running two pricing models creates operational complexity.
Pilot results may be noisy and delay a needed strategic change.
disconfirming_signals: Sales team cannot explain the model consistently.
Finance cannot compare gross retention and expansion cleanly across cohorts.

options.name: Full migration this quarter
options.description: Move all enterprise customers to platform pricing immediately.
options.expected_value: High upside, high customer and operational risk.
options.upside: Fast ARR uplift and clean strategic positioning.

options.name: Controlled pilot
options.description: Use platform pricing for selected new opportunities and one renewal cohort.
options.upside: Learns pricing power while limiting blast radius.

options.name: Keep seat-based pricing
options.description: Delay pricing change and optimize packaging within current model.
options.expected_value: Low risk but may leave value uncaptured.
options.downside: Continued mismatch between product value and monetization.

evidence.claim: Enterprise value is increasingly tied to workflow coverage rather than active seats.
evidence.source: Sales call notes and customer success QBRs.
evidence.notes: Needs systematic tagging across recent enterprise conversations.

evidence.claim: Full migration could create renewal friction.
evidence.source: Customer success feedback from price increase conversations.
evidence.notes: Risk is highest for customers with low current usage maturity.

evidence.claim: A pilot can produce useful conversion and ACV data within one quarter.
evidence.source: Current enterprise pipeline volume and renewal calendar.
evidence.notes: Sample size should be checked before launch.

risks.mitigation: Create a one-page pricing narrative, calculator, and discount guardrails.
Start with new deals and a narrow renewal cohort.
Define finance reporting before pilot launch.

decision_criteria: Pilot improves ACV without materially extending sales cycle.
Customers can understand the value metric.
Finance can report new and old pricing cohorts cleanly.
what_would_change_my_mind: Pilot customers reject platform pricing without meaningful ACV uplift.
Billing or reporting changes consume more than one sprint.
open_questions: Which value metric is simplest and most defensible?
How many renewal accounts should be included?
What discount authority should sales have?
next_actions: Build finance model for three pricing bands.
Draft sales narrative and customer FAQ.

success_metrics: Pilot ACV versus comparable seat-based opportunities.
review_questions: Did the new model explain value better?
Should the model be rolled out, revised, or stopped?
